steps.title: Understanding x-intercepts of a rational function
steps.content: For $g(x) = \frac{f(x)}{x-19}$ to have an $x$-intercept, we need $g(x) = 0$. A rational function equals zero when its numerator is zero AND its denominator is non-zero. So we need $f(x) = 0$ with $x \neq 19$.

steps.title: Categorizing the 9 zeros of f
steps.content: The function $f$ has exactly 9 $x$-intercepts. One is at $x = 19$ where $f(19) = 0$. The other 8 zeros of $f$ occur at values where $x \neq 19$.

steps.title: Counting x-intercepts of g
steps.content:
At $x = 19$: We have $f(19) = 0$ and $(x-19) = 0$, creating $\frac{0}{0}$, a removable discontinuity (hole), not an $x$-intercept.
At the other 8 zeros of $f$: The numerator $f(x) = 0$ while the denominator $(x-19) \neq 0$, so $g(x) = 0$, these ARE $x$-intercepts.
Therefore, $g(x)$ has 8 $x$-intercepts.
